claim_chain: Handwashing promotion reduces diarrhoea and respiratory infections substantially
Evidence for cost-effectiveness exists but is scarce and of wide-ranging quality
Studies that successfully increase and sustain handwashing behaviours are very likely to be cost-effective
Multiple methodological gaps exist including lack of respiratory infection valuation and adoption modeling
More empirical studies with primary data collection are needed
Results can inform resource allocation and prioritization decisions

typical_pattern: Present supporting evidence with numerical ranges or comparisons
Provide context by comparing to other studies or thresholds
Identify gaps or future research needs

likely_priorities: Quality and rigor of economic evaluation methods
Completeness of outcome measurement including respiratory infections
Modeling of behavior adoption and adherence over time
Generalizability across different settings and populations
Transparency in reporting of assumptions and limitations
likely_attacks: Limited number of high-quality empirical studies with primary data
Uncertainty about appropriate cost-effectiveness thresholds for different countries
Age of cost data and uncertainty in inflation adjustment
Use of CHEERS for reporting quality rather than actual study quality
Lack of studies comparing multiple handwashing promotion interventions
reviewer_preference_profile.inference_basis: The paper explicitly discusses multiple limitations including that 'evidence is scarce and of wide-ranging quality', 'there remains much debate on which cost-effectiveness thresholds are appropriate', 'we focussed on handwashing with soap and excluded ABHR', 'some cost data underlying our main findings are approaching 20–30 years old', and 'CHEERS does measure quality of reporting rather than actual study or model quality'. The authors also identify specific literature gaps including that 'more empirical studies with primary data collection are required', 'no diarrhoea-oriented study in a L&MIC also valued reductions in respiratory diseases', and 'only one study compared two or more interventions promoting handwashing with soap'.

examples.quoted_phrase: promotion of handwashing with soap in domestic, school, and childcare settings is very likely to be cost-effective for interventions that successfully increase and sustain adoption of handwashing behaviours
evidence is scarce and of wide-ranging quality, so this interpretation remains uncertain
this uncertainty over appropriate thresholds means our conclusions about cost-effectiveness cannot be as clear-cut as we would like
Promoting handwashing with soap reduces risk of diarrhoea by 30% and respiratory infections by 17%

verification_status.notes: All extracted phrases are literal substrings from the input text. No external references, author names, or invented numbers were included. The profile is based entirely on the provided abstract, discussion, and figure legends.
